data.title: 2 High-Yielding Dividend Picks To Fund Your Retirement
data.description:
I have been quite actively covering high quality BDCs, equity REITs and MLPs that offer decent dividend yields with all of the necessary financial properties in place to mitigate the risk of distribution cut.

The main focus point with these kinds of assets is to facilitate a slow and steady income compounding process that would, ultimately, once the retirement age kicks in, develop into a durable income portfolio, which could, in turn, fund a large portion of living expenses.

To make this happen, there has to be an emphasis not only on yield but also on above-inflation level growth that is underpinned by truly robust financials.

However, some investors might already from the start prefer high yield and safety without the income growth component due to, for example, a need to immediately spend the current income streams or just magnify the capital for making periodic reinvestments. Similarly, there could also be a merit of including such securities to enhance the overall portfolio yield and stimulate diversification, while still keeping in place the objective of durable and attractive income growth.

With this in mind, let me now present two high-yielding investment picks, which, in my opinion, could be easily included in retirement income seeking portfolios.

Pick #1 - Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC) is an actively managed closed end fund, providing a monthly yield of 9.8%. It has a clear strategy to deploy capital into mostly preferred securities and to a smaller degree in other below senior debt or bond fixed income instruments such as hybrid bonds and convertibles.

Already here there is a benefit of accessing current income streams, which are stipulated in financing agreements and are more protected than stocks. Granted, we are not talking about senior or investment grade bonds, which could be considered an even safer play, but still, due to massive exposure to preferred shares, JPC is less volatile from an income perspective than similar funds with pure play equity investments.

Speaking of the risks, they are further mitigated through the following two factors:

  • JPC is diversified across ~ 345 securities, where the Top 5 accounts only for 7.5% of the total exposure.

  • This is very critical to appreciate - about 80% of the total exposure is placed in investment grade or upper investment grade investments, which materially de-risk the overall position. The current exposure to IG securities is clearly above the Fund's mandate to hold at least 50% of such assets.

Finally, one could theoretically make a case against JPC for being included in retirement portfolios by pinpointing to the presence of external leverage, which, as of now, explains roughly 37% of the Fund's asset base.

However, when it comes to external leverage, usually, the key risks lie within the cost of debt levels, when the financing has been assumed at below market level interest rates. It means that once such borrowings get refinanced at higher interest rates, there is automatically a notable pressure put on the existing distributions (with a high chance of leading to a distribution cut).

The good news in JPC's case is that the current cost of debt stands at ~ 6.3%, which is largely reflective of the prevailing market level financing rates. So, going forward, the chances of JPC announcing a reduction in distributions due to ballooning debt service costs are extremely limited, especially considering the current normalization process in base rate levels.

All in all, JPC is a safe choice for investors, who want to maximize the yield level already from the start, while not putting a heavy emphasize on income growth, which the Fund cannot deliver in a meaningful fashion due to inherent limitations that come from preferred security exposures (i.e., fixed income like dynamics).

Pick #2 - Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF

The Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) is an even higher yielding monthly pick, which currently offers a dividend of 11.35%. At its core, QYLD is a pure play covered call ETF, which sells options against the Nasdaq-100 index in order to enhance the yield potential.

Before I explain in a more detail why QYLD could be deemed as an enticing pick to boost current income streams now, let's first understand how the covered call mechanics work:

  1. QYLD goes long the Nasdaq 100 index, which initially replicates the full set of risks and returns that would come from a simple investment in the index.
  2. Then QYLD sells calls against its long exposure to Nasdaq 100. This way, QYLD collects premiums that are associated with these sold call options.
  3. The calls are sold out of the money, which means that not the entire upside is limited. Namely, there is usually some value that QYLD can capture from a rising index.
  4. If the index surpasses the option strike value that was sold by QYLD, any gains above that point are lost (i.e., not captured by QYLD unitholders).
  5. If the index declines, QYLD keeps the option premiums, which help offset all or part of the registered downside at the index level.

Another important element here to recognize is that the key driver for option premiums (i.e., amounts QYLD receives from selling covered calls) is the level of VIX or implied market volatility. Namely, the higher uncertainty in the markets, the more money QYLD received, which it can then distribute in higher dividends.

The worst case what could happen is if the markets are calm and the Nasdaq-100 slowly but surely declines in value.

Yet, in my humble opinion, this is a very unlikely scenario. It is hard to imagine how the large cap names such as Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) or Apple (AAPL) could lose their market cap levels without the overall Nasdaq-100 index not experiencing elevated volatility levels.

In other words, we could think of QYLD as a quasi fixed income instrument, which has a range bound payoff profile with the underlying yields subject to volatility, where even in the worst-case scenario, the dividend would remain fairly attractive.

This is what we can also see in the 5-year dividend distribution chart below. The lowest dividend yield in this time period was ~9%, when the VIX was extremely depressed, and the market was trading sideways.

In fact, if we look at the historical 5-year VIX average levels, we will see rather low and stable levels throughout the entire period, in which QYLD has still managed to deliver truly enticing dividends to its shareholders.

Given the above, it would be fair to conclude that QYLD could fit nicely into high income-seeking portfolios, where the investors are agnostic to price volatility, and instead are mostly focused on capturing abnormal dividends to boost their portfolio overall yield levels.

The bottom line

While for many income investors, accessing reasonable dividend growth is necessary to maximize the compounding process, there could be a case for sacrificing growth to achieve truly abnormal current income streams.

For example, it could make sense to allocate into safe high income producing assets if the goal is to already from the start rely on portfolio income or just tactically boost the overall portfolio yield level. Accessing attractive dividends, especially as in the case of JPC and QYLD, which distribute them on a monthly basis, can even nicely complement periodic contributions to the portfolio.

Based on the financial details above around JPC and QYLD, I would feel comfortable including both of these names into a retirement income portfolio in order to enhance the monthly income levels.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase May Face Some Macro Headwinds
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) (NEOE:JPM:CA) reported a strong q3'24 driven by noninterest income growing by 11% and net income growth of 3%. As net interest margins set course to decline, the bank offset this with fees primarily driven by card-related income. As macroeconomic uncertainty continues with improving macroeconomic data and lower expectations for rate cuts for the duration of 2024, JPM will likely remain in an easing position of growth as NII expectations are set for continued decline in eFY25. Given these factors, I rate JPM shares with a HOLD rating and a price target of $230/share at 2x price/book.

JPMorgan Chase Operations

Consumer & Community Banking

JPM experienced a -3% year-over-year decline in total net revenue in the CCB segment as a result of a -6% decline in net interest income and a -6% decline in mortgage fees & related income. Mortgage revenue was likely the result of the softening real estate market, as mortgage rates remain relatively elevated. Management mentioned in their q3'24 earnings call that the bank experienced a pickup in mortgage applications and refinancing.

Revenue declines were offset by card income, which grew 19% on a year-over-year basis, and asset management fees, which were up 15% from the previous year. The bank grew its provisions for credit losses by 93% from the previous year to $2,795mm.

Though home lending experienced an up shift in revenue by 3%, average subsegment loans declined by 5%. The bank has increased its home lending loans held-for-sale to $8.4b in q3'24, up from $4.7b in q1'24.

Net charge-offs also grew by 37% to $1,919mm across all sub segments, with card services being the driving factor, increasing by 44%. Auto loans charge-offs also grew by 13% to $113mm, with a 30+ day delinquency rate of 1.23%. In terms of total delinquencies and charge-offs, management believes that the increases in these figures are more normalization to historical averages as opposed to a growing concern.

Looking at historical FRED data, the figures appear to be above 2019 levels; however, when looking further back, figures are more aligned with 2006 rates.

Commercial & Investment Bank

CIB revenue grew by 8% on a year-over-year basis to $17b, driven by IB fees, which grew by 31% to $2.3b. IB fees were primarily driven by $1,076mm in debt underwriting, a 56% increase on a year-over-year basis. Equity grew by 26% from the previous year. In terms of the outlook, management provided a relatively conservative view given the regulatory environment for M&A and growing geopolitical tension. Management is seeing softness in new loan demand and revolver utilization despite an appealing capital markets environment for middle market and large corporate clients.

Asset & Wealth Management

JPM grew AUM by 23% on a year-over-year basis to $3,904B in q3'24 and total client assets to $5,721b. The bulk of the growth in the segment derived from private banking, growing 33% on a year-over-year basis. Looking at investment classes, fixed income was the largest share of asset migration. Equity experienced a 40% year-over-year growth rate as asset values continued to improve. Customers also appear to be moving more into liquidity, potentially to preserve dry powder, as equity valuations remain elevated.

JPMorgan Chase Financials

Given the current macroeconomic environment, I'm forecasting net interest income to come in at $92.5b for eFY24, driven by a slight contraction in net interest margins.

This should translate to total revenue coming in at $178b for eFY24, for a diluted EPS of $18.82/share. These figures fall into the higher end of analyst estimates for eFY24 at $169-172b in revenue and a diluted EPS of $16.95-19.95/share.

Macroeconomic Factors

Management suggested in the q3'24 earnings call that the firm is extending the duration in order to hedge the risk of additional rate cuts. The yield curve has significantly contorted as the 10-year yield sits above the 2-year; however, the 3-month yield still remains ~55bps above the 10-year yield. Comparing the current curve to the last two years, the curve appears to have flattened, though still remains inverted.

Management suggested that further cuts to the Federal Funds rate can create a headwind for the bank; however, a steeper yield curve will be beneficial to the bank. In terms of capital raises, looser monetary policy can be seen as beneficial for enterprises to raise capital through debt issuances and refinancing of existing debt and be less dependent on equity issuances. Holding all else equal, looser monetary policy can help push up equity and fixed income values given this factor, providing an appealing scenario for JPM's asset management segment.

Economists had initially forecast another 25-50bps cut at the November meeting; however, improving economic data may push the Fed to declaring no additional cuts for the duration of 2024. The September CPI print came in at 2.4% year-over-year growth, down from 2.5% in August.

US unemployment also remains tame. I believe that this is driven by employers seeking to both retain current employees and limiting hiring to manage down costs.

Given that the Fed is using current data as direction for future cuts, I believe that these factors may lead to a 0-25bps cut in November.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

JPM shares currently trade at 1.92x price/book value to peer megabanks. This valuation may be justified given the financial strength and diverse banking services JPM has to offer, allowing for the bank to assuage any major swings in the CIB segment.

In q3'24, JPM reduced the share count by -2.26%; I believe management will continue to opportunistically buy back shares when appropriate. Jamie Dimon, mentioned on the earnings call:

If you look at it roughly, we have about a minimum $30 billion of excess capital. I look at it as you own the whole company and you can't properly deploy it now is perfectly reasonable to wait. We will be able to deploy it. Our shareholders will be very well-served by just waiting… buying stock back at more than two times tangible book value is not necessarily the best thing to do, because we think we'll have better opportunities to redeploy it or to buy back at cheaper prices at one point. Markets do not stay high forever.

Jamie Dimon

Given the high valuation JPM shares hold and the uncertainty of the macroeconomic environment ahead, I will rate JPM shares with a HOLD rating. Though shares have traded as high as 2.02x price/book in the last 12 months, I do believe that the current valuation makes JPM shares less appealing as an investor considering building a position. At 2x price/book, JPM shares can trade as high as $230/share.

data.title: Sandstorm Gold Offers Unique Growth Potential Amid Record Gold Prices
data.description:
Introduction

As gold prices have risen, reaching record highs surpassing $2600, investors in gold miners have been rewarded, with the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) up 30.6% year to date. However, buying miners or the underlying commodity need not be the only way to gain exposure to the rising gold price. Royalty and Streaming companies, such as Sandstorm Gold (NYSE:SAND) (TSX:SSL:CA), offer investors a unique way to gain exposure to the precious metal market. Unlike traditional mining companies, Sandstorm doesn’t operate mines, but instead generates revenue from holding royalties and streams on various assets in exchange for an upfront payment. With a portfolio spanning over 200 streams and royalties, across 41 producing underlying mines, Sandstorm offers unrivalled diversification.

With a focus on precious metals, Sandstorm is set to benefit from the rising price of gold and silver. The past 5 years have not been the best for shareholders with the shares down 0.3% in the period; however, the shares have followed the gold price higher this year, up 15.51% so far. With a high gold price supported by central bank buying, rising geopolitical tensions, and interest rate cuts, combined with rising industrial demand for silver, I believe Sandstorm is in a strong position. With a diversified portfolio of generating assets and a strong future development pipeline, I believe Sandstorm Gold offers a compelling opportunity for precious metal investors.

Q2 Results

Sandstorm Gold released its Q2 results at the start of August, reporting quarterly sales of 17,416 gold equivalent ounces (GEOs), a decrease from the 24,504 GEOs reported the previous year. This fall in gold equivalent ounces was driven by the expected end of the gold and silver streams at the Mercedes gold mine, a decrease in head grades at the Cerro Moro Silver mine, and a geotechnical event at the Aurizona mine which led to a pause in mining. The decrease in GEOs was partially offset by the 56% increase in the number of pounds of copper sold from the Chapada copper stream. Full-year production is forecast to be between 75,000 and 85,000 GEOs, with Sandstorm forecasting production to reach 125,000 GEOs in the next five years.

Looking closer at the financials, revenue came in at $41.4 million, a decrease from the $49.8 million reported in 2023, a fall of 16.9%, driven by lower GEO volume. Despite the fall in GEOs of 27.8%, this was partially offset by a much higher realized average gold price of $2,313 compared to $1,972 a year earlier. With a significantly higher gold price and average cash costs only up $52 to $270 from a year ago, the company reported a cash operating margin of $2,042, a new record for the company and equivalent to a cash margin of over 88% consistent with the company’s long-term goal of maintaining industry-leading cost efficiency.

Operating cash flow for the quarter came in at $34.4 million with net income at $10.5 million, compared to $42.1 million and $2.7 million in the previous year. This resulted in a net income of $0.03 per share. For the first half of 2024, Sandstorm generated $65.1 million in cash flow from operating activity, and the company expects to continue growing its cash flow in the future to reach $160 million in full-year cash flow in 2025, and, with the MARA streaming options, $250 million in 2029, assuming there are no further delays at its key projects.

The company ended the quarter with $17.9 million in cash and short-term investments, with total debt at $388 million. Since its debt financed acquisitions in 2022, the company has reduced its debts considerably, from a high of $526.2 million in September 2022 to $388 million presently. This debt reduction has enabled Sandstorm to start performing share buybacks, repurchasing 457,125 shares for $2.5 million in the first half of 2024.

With the gold price having continued its upward trend, the company looks set to post a solid third quarter, with revenue estimated to increase 6.3% year-on-year to reach $43.94 million, and earnings per share forecast at $0.03 compared to $0.01 in Q3 2023.

Key Developments

With a portfolio spanning over 200 streams and royalties, across 41 producing underlying mines, it would take too long to go through everyone, so I will highlight the key developments for the company and future outlook. This many royalties and streams mean the company has incredible diversification, with no asset more than 10% of its NAV and no country greater than 14%, significantly reducing jurisdictional risk and reliance on a single project which many smaller mining companies face. The company focuses primarily on precious metals, with 74% of revenues expected to come from gold and silver in 2025, rising to 85% in 2029.

Full-year production is forecast to be between 75,000 and 85,000 GEOs, with Sandstorm forecasting production to reach 125,000 GEOs in the next five years. This growth is driven by many of its development assets beginning production. Currently, 62% of its portfolio NAV is in production and by 2029 this is set to rise to 89%. This is driven by a pipeline of developments set to come online over the next five years.

Greenstone

The Greenstone project, located in Ontario, is progressing smoothly towards full commercial production, with the first gold pour achieved in May 2024, producing 1,800 ounces from its full recovery circuit. Over the mine’s 14-year lifespan, it is expected to produce 5 million ounces of gold, with an average annual output of 400,000 ounces in the first five years. Sandstorm is entitled to 2.375% of gold produced until 120,333 ounces are delivered, when it then becomes entitled to 1.583%. Sandstorm will make ongoing payments of 20% of the spot price plus a $30 ESG contribution for this gold. With the mine only beginning to ramp up operations, this will help drive revenues significantly higher in the coming years.

Greenstone’s significant scale, with 135.3 Million tonnes of reserves containing 5.54 Moz of gold at 1.27 g/t grade, and with the low forecast, All In Sustaining Costs (AISC) places it in the lowest cost quartile of gold producers globally. Potential future expansions, including underground development, are not currently factored into the mine plan, providing future potential for Sandstorm’s royalty stream. Now fully owned by Equinox Gold (EQX), and with the mine beginning to ramp up in 2025, I believe Greenstone will be a key asset in Sandstorm’s portfolio, providing long-term cashflow and royalty growth.

Antamina

The Antamina mine, located in the Peruvian Andes, is the third-largest copper mine globally and represents an estimated 6% of Sandstorm Gold’s net asset value. The mine produces around 560,000 tonnes of copper equivalent per year and has a processing capacity of 145,000 tonnes per day. Sandstorm Gold holds a Net Profits Interest Royalty of 0.55%, and a 1.66% silver stream at 2.5% of the spot price which provided Sandstorm with $1.3 million of operating cash flow in Q2 2024.

The mine contains over 226 million tonnes of reserves at a grade of 0.94% copper, 0.56% zinc, and 10.7 g/t silver. With the mine life recently extended to 2036 following the approval of a $2 billion investment in mine optimization, this means Sandstorm can continue to benefit from this asset, generating stable royalty and stream income.

Hod Maden

The Hod Maden project, located in North-Eastern Turkey, is a gold-copper development project, on which Sandstorm holds a 20% gold stream at 50% of the spot price until 405,000 ounces are delivered, after which it transitions to a 12% stream at 60% of spot. It also holds a 2% net smelter return royalty on the project.

While the mine may not be online for a fair number of years yet, with an expected 2 million gold ounces expected to be produced over the mine's initial 13-year lifetime, the mine, when in production, will provide significant cash flow to Sandstorm. The 2021 Feasibility Study suggested an AISC of $334 per ounce of gold and a pre-tax NPV of $1.3 billion at a 5% discount rate, with a 41% internal rate of return. The mine has an expected average annual production of 195,000 ounces of gold equivalent at an 11.1 g/t head grade, which is expected to have a significant impact on Sandstorm’s GEOs produced. Over the first four years, it will contribute over 36,000 GEOs annually to Sandstorm, giving a large boost to the company’s annual sales and cash flows when operational. To put things into perspective, Sandstorm is predicting 80,000 GEOs for the entirety of 2024, suggesting this mine will have a major positive impact on Sandstorm. The mine is fully permitted for construction, with early works underway.

MARA

The MARA project, located in Argentina, is one of the largest undeveloped copper-gold assets in the world with total reserves reaching 11.8 billion pounds of copper and 7.4 million ounces of gold. Sandstorm holds a 0.25% Net Smelter Return royalty on the mine, with the option to convert the 0.25% royalty into a gold stream for 20% of the gold produced at the mine for the entire mine life at 30% of the spot price. This option requires the advance payment of between $135 and $225 million. The open pit mine is predicted to have a life of 28 years, with an average yearly production of 556 million pounds of copper and 107,000 ounces of gold. Sandstorm’s $225 million purchase price for this stream will only be paid once a construction decision has been made. Although Argentina currently has a more business-friendly president than previous administrations, it is important to note that mining remains a matter for the province in which it is located for permitting. While the construction decision remains pending, if mining does commence, MARA represents a significant long-term opportunity for Sandstorm.

In summary, with a diverse portfolio of producing mines and multiple potential development projects, Sandstorm Gold has a massive runway for growth and the potential to provide increasing returns to shareholders over the coming years.

Royalty Companies and a Rising Gold Price

Why would a mining company seeking to develop a mine sacrifice a portion of their revenues or production by picking a royalty company over traditional equity or debt financing. The simple reason is that when a royalty company like Sandstorm provides upfront capital to a mining company in exchange for a stream or royalty, it allows the mining company to avoid issuing new shares (that would dilute shareholders) or the issuance of debt, which can burden the balance sheet. Unlike debt, royalty payments are tied directly to production, so if a mine faces delays or lower than expected output, the mining company isn’t required to pay fixed interest or principal payments.

While both traditional mining companies and royalty companies are affected by changes in the price of the underlying commodity, royalty companies like Sandstorm Gold offer significant benefits. The most important is the reduced operational risk, such as inflationary cost pressures and capital expenditure overruns. Another key benefit is the vast diversification offered. It would take billions in capital for a mining firm to build a diversified portfolio of mines, but for a royalty company, a much smaller amount can gain exposure to a huge number of mines. Sandstorm Gold has royalties on over 43 operational mines insulating it from issues at a singular mine and a singular jurisdiction. By acquiring royalties and streams, Sandstorm Gold benefits from the production and sale of gold from a diverse portfolio of mines whilst remaining insulated from daily operational challenges.

The cash Sandstorm does generate is typically at very high margins. In Q2 2024, the company recorded a margin of $2043 per ounce due to the higher average realized gold price of $2,313, and average cash cost of only $270 an ounce. This is driven by the company’s ability to collect a percentage of the mine’s revenue or production regardless of the underlying costs of mining. This means that even in an inflationary environment that drives up a mine's cost, Sandstorm’s margins remain stable, enabling more consistent returns to shareholders than producers.

Gold prices have risen this year, reaching a record high of over $2600 an ounce, driven by geopolitical tensions, central bank buying, and the prospects of falling interest rates. I covered these in more detail in my previous article on the VanEck Gold Miners ETF. Central banks have been significant buyers of gold as they diversify their reserves away from the US dollar with a record 483 tonnes of gold purchased in the first half of 2024 and 69% of central banks expecting gold to make up an increasing part of their reserves over the next five years. With ongoing conflicts across the world and rising geopolitical risks, gold continues to remain a favored safe-haven asset, supporting continued price strength.

For a royalty company like Sandstorm Gold, these rising gold prices directly translate into higher cash flow. As gold prices rise, Sandstorm’s revenue from royalties and streaming rises in tandem with the gold price, aiding earnings. This is because many of its contracts are in the form of a percentage of the net smelter revenue or a stream purchased at a fixed price or a percentage of the spot price. With a large, diversified portfolio, Sandstorm Gold stands to benefit from this higher gold price. Combined with the fact the company has little direct exposure to mining costs, I believe the company’s model will enable it to perform strongly into the future.

Valuation

Placing a valuation on a gold royalty company is hard, given their earnings reliance on the price of gold. In a rising gold market, all precious metal royalty companies stand to benefit from a higher gold price, but royalty firms have different valuations which may impact potential future returns. As such, I believe one way to value the company is in comparison to other precious metal royalty companies such as Osisko Gold (OR), Royal Gold (RGLD), Franco-Nevada (FNV), and Triple Flag Precious Metals (TFPM). Given the large difference in cash and debt levels, a direct comparison of earnings multiples will not suffice, and I instead use an enterprise value to EBITDA multiple.

When compared to its peers, we see that Sandstorm Gold trades at a significant discount with a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 15,52, the lowest amongst its peers. Given the large diversification the company offers, with no singular mine or jurisdiction representing more than 10% of operations, and the development pipeline in the coming years, I believe that on this metric the company appears undervalued.

Mining companies are often valued using a price to net asset value approach. I believe royalty companies like Sandstorm Gold warrant a different approach. Exploration and development companies derive most of their value from assets they can prove, whereas royalty and streaming companies depend on the cash flow that arises from the production and sale of mineral ounces from a mine. Therefore, I believe that comparing the price to free cash flow metric is a key metric to determine whether Sandstorm Gold is undervalued or overvalued relative to its peers.

Again, Sandstorm Gold trades at a significant discount to its peers. As Sandstorm continues to paydown its debt and buyback shares, I would expect the shares to rerate to at least trade in-line with peers, suggesting at least a 45% upside on a price to free cash flow metric to trade at a similar level to Triple Flag. This implies a share price of $8.42. Combined with the potential for further upside as full commercial production commences at Greenstone and growing production at Amina, Sandstorm Gold is well positioned. Combined with the rising gold price, and potential for production from MARA mine significantly increasing production, I believe the shares are a strong buy.

Risks

Despite my positive view on the company, investing in Sandstorm Gold is not without its risks. I believe the biggest risk is its dependence on the price of gold and the other metals it has royalties and streams on. Whilst I remain bullish on the price of gold in the long term, the price could fall if some of the underlying drivers; central bank gold buying, rising geopolitical tensions and interest rate reductions, reverse. Given the company’s revenues and earnings dependence on the price of the underlying metal, any changes in the price are likely to impact both the company and its share price.

Another important risk to consider is that of mine development delays and the possibility that some assets in Sandstorm’s portfolio never reach production. If a mining operator encounters issues, such as permitting delays, technical problems, or local opposition, it can delay or even halt production, reducing the expected cash flows from Sandstorm’s royalties or streams. Additionally, as a royalty firm, Sandstorm does not have operational control over the mining companies it finances, despite its fate being tied to how well the mining company performs. Should a mining company choose to alter production plans or shift resources to different areas, Sandstorm may see its expected revenues fall, as royalty payments can often depend on production levels from specific areas of a mine. This lack of control is something investors need to consider when investing in Sandstorm Gold.

Conclusion

Sandstorm Gold offers an attractive investment opportunity. Outside of the upside provided by rising precious metal prices, Sandstorm has an improving fundamental picture with the Greenstone mine set to go into full production next year, and the potential for further upside from development projects like MARA. With an attractive valuation compared to its peers, and debt being reduced, the company is in a good position. Combined with share buybacks, I believe the shares are a strong buy at this price.

data.title: Bank of America Q3 Earnings: Net Interest Income Finally Rose
data.description:
Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) reported Q3 earnings on Tuesday. Unlike peers such as JPMorgan (JPM) and Wells Fargo (WFC), which soared after reporting earnings, BAC's stock started the trading day with a slight increase of 0.12%.

On a one-year basis, Bank of America has slightly outperformed its peers with a 58.17% price return, but with the other major banks also gaining above the 50% mark.

In my previous analysis of BAC, I rated them as a "buy," especially after considering that management believed that Q2 was the net interest income trough. Three months later, management was right, as net interest income grew 2% sequentially. Still, there is some progress to be made, as the downward trend has yet to revert for the year, as NII remained -3% lower.

Considering this, I will revisit my "buy" rating on BAC by analyzing the key highlights in Q3, together with the new valuation, risks, and market outlook.

Bank of America: Actual Numbers vs. Analyst Expectations

Let's start with where earnings sat versus what analysts expected. On this occasion, most items were beaten, including EPS, revenue, and net interest income. Although the latter two were not beaten by a large differential, EPS sat 6.6% above consensus. Despite 6.6% being a decent margin, it was not as appealing compared to the 9.5% beat of JPM and 10.1% of WFC. Last, the only line item missed was the provision for credit losses, just by a tiny 0.65%.

BAC: Q3 Earnings at a Glance

Although net interest income rose sequentially, this wasn't true for non-interest income, which declined by -2.5%. Yet on a yearly basis, the gains from non-interest income more than offset the declines in NII, making total revenue rise by a slight 0.7%.

Net income was flat sequentially and -11.6% down for the year. In addition, the effect of $3.5 billion share repurchases couldn't soften the declines, and diluted EPS saw a -2.4% drop QoQ and -10.0% YoY.

Moving to the balance sheet, US non-interest-bearing deposits continued to decline $4.8 billion from Q2. Yet, interest-bearing deposits saw a small gain of $17.0 billion, bringing total deposits up 2.4% year-over-year. At the same time, the loan book (net of allowances) rose slightly to 1.8% QoQ and 2.6% YoY. Concerning unrealized losses on debt securities, this item improved sequentially, passing from $114.8 billion to $89.4 billion.

Furthermore, the common equity tier 1 capital ratio decreased by a modest ten bps to 11.8%, but it was still comfortably above the new 10.7% minimum requirement as of October 1.

Finally, the yield on earning assets was basically flat at 1.92%, but with early signs of slight decreases in yields on interest-earning liabilities and net charge-offs of 3.70% improved 18 bps from the preceding quarter.

Bank of America: NII Growth Expected again in Q4

During the earnings call, management reiterated that they were right on the NII flip, as previously guided, and seemed pretty confident that the same will happen in Q4. At the same time, they signaled that they were quick to adjust the deposit yield on their Global Banking and Wealth business following the 50 bps rate cut in September. Nonetheless, these moves just materialized by a small portion since the rate cut was close to the end of the quarter. Furthermore, BAC expects yields within those segments to fall below 2% later in October.

In addition, the bank is seeing a good deposit growth outlook, with Global Baking returning to normal seasonality, Wealth flattening, and Consumer Banking slowing in withdrawals and in place of finding a floor. The same is true regarding loan growth, as there was some of it this quarter, and credit line utilizations remain below pre-pandemic levels.

BAC: Price to TVB Became Cheaper in Q3

With a tangible book value per share of $26.25 and a closing price of $42.14, the current price-to-tangible book is at 1.61x. This is an improvement from three months ago when the post-earnings valuation stood at 1.74x, but obviously higher than a year ago, when banks were cheap, and BAC's post-earnings valuation sat at 1.16x. Nonetheless, a price-to-tangible book of 1.61x sits within the historical five-year average of 1.56x.

In addition, considering that the dividend yield remains close to its historical level and that the dividend has been exhibiting a five-year CAGR of 9.24%, the stock remains at an attractive entry point at the $40 level.

Non-Interest Income Slow Down Risk

Gains in non-interest income through 2024 have helped alleviate the NII headwinds of money center banks. Yet, compared with peers, Bank of America is clearly underperforming, with non-interest income even lower for the quarter.

(in million $) Q3 '24 Q3 '23 YoY
Investment banking fees BAC 1,403 1,188 18.1%
Investment banking fees WFC 668 545 22.6%
Investment banking fees JPM 2,267 1,729 31.1%
Market making BAC 3,278 3,325 -1.4%
Trading activities WFC 1,366 1,193 14.5%
Markets & Securities JPM 8,369 7,768 7.7%

Source: BAC, JPM, WFC Supplemental Information Q3

For example, regarding investment banking fees and market-making activity, BAC remains third on the list, as displayed in the table above. This is not a minor issue since BAC's non-interest income was down QoQ in Q3, and a slowdown of non-interest income would alter NII tailwinds. Especially due to BAC's mixed income composition, which as of Q3, had a fair distribution of 55% NII and 45% non-II.

Takeaway

With the initial numbers of net interest income recovery, a slightly cheaper cost on interest-earning liabilities, and robust asset quality ratios, Bank of America's future looks bright over the next year as long as things do not unfold in the economy. Despite non-interest income growth underperforming peers such as JPM and WFC (especially in investment banking), it still has ample room to expand further. Even more so when considering the dovish monetary policy.

These earnings, in conjunction with a valuation that even decreased and sits at normalized levels, make me reiterate my "buy" rating on Bank of America.

data.title: Interactive Brokers: Growing Introducing Broker Business; Initiate With 'Buy'
data.description:
Interactive Brokers (NASDAQ:IBKR) has been growing their introducing broker business, attracting more brokers to its platform. Interactive Brokers is able to provide competitive pricing to their customers thanks to their business automation. I am initiating with a ‘Buy’ rating with a one-year target price of $190 per share.

Growing Introducing Broker Business

In June 2024, HSBC (HSBC) launched their WorldTrader, a new digital investment platform, in partnership with Interactive Brokers. As the introducing broker, Interactive Brokers offers automated trade execution and custody across over 150 markets. During the earnings call, the management indicated that a couple of dozen brokers were testing Interactive Brokers’ system, with the intention of using its trading and custody services in the future.

I think the introducing broker business could potentially accelerate Interactive Brokers’ growth in the future. Key benefits include:

  • These brokers, including HSBC, are large asset management companies with a substantial client base. Once Interactive Brokers becomes the introducing broker, it can earn both trading commission and custody fees. While trading commissions are volatile, custody revenue is relatively stable.
  • As an introducing broker, Interactive Brokers can provide margin loans to their clients, generating more interest income.
  • Large financial advisor and broker-dealer customers can ‘‘white brand’’ Interactive Brokers’ platform, as indicated in the 10K. In addition, Interactive Brokers can provide streamlined customer service for brokers and advisors, reducing the complexity of managing client assets.

Low-Cost Offerings Via Automation

Interactive Brokers has leveraged their automation technology to lower their operating costs and offer competitive pricing to their customers. As shown in the slide below, automation has been implemented throughout processes from account opening to the entire transaction life cycle. The automation has dramatically reduced their overall costs and improved the operating margin over time.

Thanks to the automation, their employee compensation and benefits expenses only accounted for 12% of total revenue in FY23, reduced from 15% in FY22. In addition, due to the high level of automations, Interactive Brokers is able to provide competitive pricing to their customers. As depicted in the table below, Interactive Brokers’ margin rate is the lowest among brokers.

Recent Result, Outlook, and Valuation

Interactive Brokers released their Q3 result on October 15th after the bell, reporting 19.2% net revenue growth with 72% of profit margin. My biggest takeaway from the quarter is the strong growth in customer accounts as well as customer equity balances, as detailed in the table below. The strong growth was supported by a bull equity market this year, leading to robust volume increases in options, stocks, and futures trading.

Given the superior YTD performance, I anticipate Interactive Brokers will maintain the growth momentum in Q4, achieving 20% revenue growth for the full year. Mordor Intelligence projects that e-brokerage market will grow at a CAGR of 6.5% from 2023 to 2029. I think Interactive Brokers’ business will be driven by increasing equity trading volumes and a growing customer base.

Due to their competitive pricing and low-cost operating model, Interactive Brokers has grown faster than the market growth in past years. As pointed out in their earnings call, hedge funds have increased their leverage with Interactive Brokers, boosting the company's interest income

As the Fed started to lower the interest rate, Interactive Brokers will earn less on margin loans in the near future. Meanwhile, the lower costs of margin loans might encourage clients to borrow more and trade more on Interactive Brokers’ platform.

Considering all these factors, I assume Interactive Brokers will grow their net revenue by 8% in the future, consisting of 6.5% market growth, 1% share gains and 0.5% growth from introducing broker business.

As Interactive Brokers earned 70.7% operating margin in FY23, I don’t expect significant margin expansion moving forward. I only model 10bps margin expansion driven by a reduction in employee compensation and benefits. As discussed earlier, I anticipate Interactive Brokers will continue to leverage their technology to automate more workloads, further reducing the spendings on employee compensation and benefits.

The cost of equity is calculated to be 13% assuming risk-free rate 3.6%; beta 1.34; equity risk premium 7%. I calculate the free cash flow from equity (FCFE) as follows:

Discounting all the future FCFE, the one-year target price is calculated to be $190 per share, according to my DCF model.

Key Risks

  • In September 2023, The Commodity Futures Trading Commission issued an order charging Interactive Brokers for failing to maintain and preserve records. The order requires Interactive Brokers to pay a $20 million civil monetary penalty. As indicated in the announcement, Interactive Brokers failed to prevent its employees from using unapproved communication methods, such as personal text messages and WhatsApp. The charge demonstrated that Interactive Brokers had insufficient internal control policies. In addition, in September 2023, the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) charged $35 million penalty on Interactive Brokers for recordkeeping failures.
  • Interactive Brokers has been benefiting from high-interest rate due to their margin loans. As the Fed began to cut interest rate, Interactive Brokers will likely earn less from these margin loans in the future, impacting their interest income on their P&L. As shown in the slide below, a 100bps interest rate decrease will reduce their annual net interest income by $235 million.

  • A technical issue at the New York Stock Exchange on June 3rd, 2024, cost Interactive Brokers $48 million. The technical issue caused Berkshire Hathaway’s (BRK.A) stocks to appear to lose nearly all their value during the day.

End Note

Interactive Brokers has leveraged their technology and automation capabilities to provide competitive pricing to clients while improving operating margins. Their introducing broker business is also likely to contribute to additional revenue growth. I am initiating with a ‘Buy’ rating with a one-year target price of $190 per share.

data.title: Nokia Earnings Preview: Too Early To Bet On A Recovery
data.description:
I turned cautious about the prospects for Nokia Oyj (NYSE:NOK) (OTCPK:NOKBF) at the beginning of this year, soon after the company announced a dividend hike, as I thought operating margins may come under pressure this year while growth prospects remain limited. Just as I predicted, the going has been tough for Nokia with all of its key business segments - network infrastructure, mobile networks, and cloud and network services - reporting double-digit YoY revenue declines in the second quarter. However, Nokia stock has performed decently since my previous article, up 16% compared to the 20% gains posted by the S&P 500 index. With Nokia expected to report third-quarter earnings in a couple of days, I thought of revisiting the company to determine whether its recent deals and cost-cutting initiatives would turn the tables around from a financial performance perspective.

What To Look Out For In Q3 Earnings

Nokia is likely to have suffered from the ongoing challenges faced by the mobile equipment industry in the third quarter as well. The main challenge facing this industry is the pullback in capital expenditure by leading mobile carriers around the world amid elevated interest rates and persistent inflation. For the third quarter, Zacks projects Nokia to report revenue of EUR 5.1 billion, registering a decline from the EUR 5.42 billion in revenue reported for the corresponding quarter last year. However, network infrastructure revenue is projected to increase more than 21% YoY in Q3, marking a notable improvement from the previous quarter when all subsegments of this sector (fixed networks, IP networks, and optical networks) saw YoY declines in revenue.

After returning to growth in spectacular fashion in 2022, Nokia's revenue growth has once again entered negative territory. Although the third quarter is unlikely to have changed this trajectory, I am looking forward to the management's discussion on new deals and the prospects for the mobile networks segment to determine whether Nokia will benefit from a major turnaround in customer spending patterns in the next few quarters.

Exhibit 1: Nokia revenue growth

Investors should also pay attention to the progress of Nokia's cost reduction plan in Q3 to project the path of operating margins. As a reminder, I was spooked by the possibility of a contraction in operating margins when I analyzed the company early this year. Back in October 2023, Nokia announced a cost-cutting plan targeting EUR 800 million to EUR 1.2 billion in savings by 2026. As part of this plan, Nokia aims to reduce its headcount from around 86,000 to between 72,000 and 77,000. The company ended Q2 with close to 80,000 employees. As a growth-oriented investor, I am looking forward to some guidance from management during the upcoming earnings call to assess whether these headcount reductions have the potential to reduce the company's cost base permanently without impacting projected growth.

Exhibit 2: Nokia's cost reduction plan progress as of Q2 2024

An improved operating margin resulting from scaled-down business operations is not what I want to see as a growth investor. I am not suggesting Nokia is going down this path, but more often than not, companies that struggle to grow end up focusing too much on boosting margins in the short term at the expense of long-term growth.

In addition to this, I will also pay close attention to the management's commentary on Nokia's India business. In the second quarter, the lackluster performance in India weighed down on revenue growth. Given the important role India is expected to play in boosting Nokia's network infrastructure revenue, investors should look for clues for a recovery in this market.

Exhibit 3: Nokia's regional sales in Q2

In addition to India, Nokia's management is likely to share 5G investment trends in other key markets such as Europe, Latin America, and the Asia Pacific.

Finally, I would welcome a discussion on the progress Nokia has made with some of the recent deals it secured. More on these new deals below.

New Deals Are Encouraging, But There Is More To The Story

In the third quarter, Nokia made progress by securing several high-profile deals with telecommunication service providers in a few regions. Below are some of the new deals the company secured in Q3.

  1. A 5-year partnership with AT&T (T) to modernize the fiber infrastructure of the telecom giant.
  2. A 3-year deal with Vodafone Idea Limited to expand network capabilities in India.
  3. A multi-year contract with Portugal-based MEO to upgrade its network infrastructure by enhancing MEO's Radio Access Network.
  4. A new deal with Telekom Malaysia to enhance data center connectivity.
  5. A partnership with TIM Brasil to enhance the 5G network infrastructure of the telecom carrier.

Securing these contracts, as Nokia bulls would agree, is the first step toward a growth revival. Although these deals are encouraging, Nokia still faces many execution barriers, which need to be closely evaluated before investing in the company.

There is no guarantee that 5G infrastructure spending will meaningfully increase in 2025 despite rate cuts. Even if it does, the real question is Nokia's prospects in the long run when telecom carriers would slow down spending after initial deployments.

In addition, the competition is ripe in the network infrastructure sector, with established players such as Huawei and Ericsson (ERIC) making their presence felt. Ericsson has already dented Nokia's earnings by taking a major AT&T contract away from the company. In the long run, due to competitive pressures, Nokia may never be able to achieve its long-term revenue growth and operating profit margin targets, leading to disappointing returns for investors.

Takeaway

Nokia's upcoming Q3 earnings report will shed light on its progress with cost reduction plans and the company's performance in key markets such as India. I believe the market may react positively to Q3 earnings given that the company is likely to reveal promising details about the new deals it secured during the third quarter. However, I still believe it's too early to jump on board Nokia, given that the company's long-term earnings growth projections are questionable amid increasing competition and the prospects for a deceleration in 5G infrastructure spending beyond initial deployments.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Why I'm Still Bullish, But Caution Is Advised For Uninitiated
data.description:
Introduction

Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) has been surrounded by a lot of controversies recently, so I wanted to give some comments on why I am still positive about the company but acknowledge that the risks do keep the share price depressed, and many investors are avoiding it. I still have a position in the company, as I do believe that the fundamentals of the company are solid, however, due to negative sentiment towards the company and from the perspective of a person who may not have any capital tied up in the company, it may be too risky to bet on it right now, and I would recommend holding off for now.

The last time I covered SMCI, it was hit with bad news left and right, which brought a lot of volatility to its shares. The company finally reached my entry point; since then, it has been down around 8% compared to the S&P500's (SPY) 9.5% increase.

What Keeps me invested

Firstly, the company's growth has been astounding since the beginning of the AI hype. The upcoming quarter's y/y growth also looks to be accelerating. The analysts expect the company to make $6.46B, over 200% higher than last year. Sequentially, the growth sits at around 22% The growth numbers are similar to that of AI darling NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA).

Is this achievable? That, of course, depends on the demand for liquid cooling racks and the overall demand for AI by enterprises, large and small. Recently, SMCI announced that the company already deployed 100,000 GPUs with its liquid cooling systems in many AI factories and data centers. So, it looks like there is still a lot of demand for innovative cooling solutions that SMCI is pioneering, coupled with the heavy capex spend of the major players within the cloud and AI segments, SMCI is going to continue to benefit greatly from this for a while, just as the numbers suggest. The juggernauts are spending billions on advancing their AI projects. Money is not an issue for them, which means they will continue to spend as much as possible to be ahead of the competition, and the competition is fierce.

When looking at the competition like Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE) and Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL), we can see how the AI explosion has benefitted SMCI the most. If we look at the last year or so, we can see that SMCI's revenues exploded while the competition either stagnated or saw a slight bump in growth. This tells me that the product that SMCI offers may be the superior product, as it shows from the below revenue comparisons.

The second thing I like about the company is its ability to get a product out to the market the quickest, which, of course, helped its revenue growth tremendously, as we can see above. The company has been very productive and agile when it came to taking advantage of AI and the ChatGPT revolution. The company was the quickest to design a rack specifically with AI in mind, and that gave it a head start and first-mover advantage, which, with time, has started to dissipate. However, while the competition is catching up with what SMCI has already achieved, the company is innovating further in terms of customization to its racks, which should keep it at the top of the competition. SMCI's racks are known for their customization, cost-effectiveness, efficiency, and flexibility, while the competitors may have brand recognition and better support services, SMCI will continue to lead the pack, improve further, and gain more market share.

Thirdly, the company's financial position is very good still. The company has quadrupled its cash position to $1.7B from $440M at the same time last quarter while also issuing some debt in the form of convertible notes that stood at around $1.7B. The interest expense was around $3M, which is easily covered by the company's EBIT, which stood at over $343M.

In short, I think the company's fundamentals are very solid, and the growth will remain for the next while.

The risks - what may keep others out

Of course, no company comes without risks, and that is especially true for SMCI. The company has been plagued with bad news upon bad news. It is important to consider these before thinking of starting a position, and ultimately, it is up to you whether you think the risks outweigh the rewards.

The negative sentiment really took off when a prominent short-seller, Hindenburg Research, came out with a piece on SMCI at the end of August. What completely crushed the company's sentiment was that following the report, the company said it would be delaying the 10K filing. However, SMCI's CEO Charles Liang came out with a letter a few days ago where he said that the company is continuing to innovate and production is unaffected to meet the customer demand, and also that the short-seller report "contains false or inaccurate statements about our company including misleading presentations of information", and that it will be addressed soon.

To add insult to injury, because of the short report, the DOJ is said to be setting up a probe into the company, however, it is not official just yet.

The company's margins have suffered recently, which is also a big factor in many investment firms lowering the company's PTs considerably. It is a real issue. However, the management expects margin pressures to ease by the end of FY25, citing from the Q4 transcript: "Again, we anticipate that the short-term margin pressure will ease and return to normal ranges before the end of fiscal 2025, especially when our DLC liquid cooling and Datacenter Building Block Solutions start to ship in high volume later this year."

On separate occasions at two different conferences after the FY24 earnings call, the company said that the margin hit was expected since it invested a lot of money into liquid cooling technology and, going forward, expects the company to return to 14% to 17% gross margins in the long run. This is short-term pain for long-term gain, in my opinion, and helps the company to stay on top of the game in terms of competition.

As I mentioned earlier, these are real risks, and as long as that uncertainty remains, I am expecting a lot of volatility for the remainder of the year and into 2025. Let's look at an updated valuation model.

Valuation

Now that the company has done a 10-for-1 split, I wanted to revisit the company's outlook and adjust some assumptions going forward.

For revenues, I went with a 20% CAGR over the next decade, with around 87% growth front-loaded for FY25, which then tapers off to 9% by FY34. I think this is quite a slowdown, and I think the company may do better than that. However, I will keep it conservative.

For margins, in my previous article, I went with a decent improvement in efficiency and profitability. This time around, I decided to improve gross margins by 100bps over the next decade. The reason I did this is I wanted to know what the company would be worth if it didn't manage to recover profitability and stabilize around these numbers.

I went with the company's WACC of 8.4% as my discount rate and a 2.5% terminal growth rate. To account for errors in the estimates above, I am also attaching another 15% discount to its final intrinsic value calculation, which gives me a fair value of $56.5 a share, meaning if the company doesn't improve its margins going forward and its revenue growth drops considerably, the company is still trading below its fair value.

Closing Comments

Ultimately, it is up to you what you think the company is worth, if anything. You may think that because of all these allegations, the company is worthless, and I get that. However, I remain bullish on the company because of the growth potential in the future that seems to be still accelerating and its solid fundamentals. The reason I am also more positive about the company is that I have not put in too much capital (yet), and for the money that is involved, I am fine with waiting it out a little more to see how this story develops.

I think the company's fundamentals should be at the forefront of its valuation. The amazing top-line growth and eventual recovery of margins will drive the company's valuation in the long run, while controversies will present better entry points in the short run. Therefore, if you are looking to start a position in SMCI, I recommend holding off for now and waiting for more news regarding everything. I am not adding any more to my position, however, if it drops below $40 a share on no new events, I will be tempted to add a little more.

data.title: REITs: Very Bond-Like
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Most investors look at REITs for yield, as bond-like, says Alex Pettee, who discusses the nuances and axioms of investing in the public and private side of real estate investment trusts with Brad Thomas and David Auerbach (1:50). Rates up, REITs down; ignore day-to-day movements (3:30). REIT valuations, property sectors and rate sensitivities (11:30). Common vs preferred shares (14:20). Bullish housing case intact? (16:00) Hurricane effects on real estate (21:00). 3 REITs worth looking at (24:10). This was originally published as a webinar on October 10.

Subscribe to iREIT + HOYA Capital

Transcript

Daniel Snyder: Hey everyone. Daniel Snyder here from Seeking Alpha. We're going to go ahead and jump into this full hour with a powerhouse panel as you see here today.

We have David, we have Alex, we have Brad Thomas, all joining us from iREIT and Hoya Capital here on Seeking Alpha. I’ll go ahead and hand it off to you, David.

David Auerbach: I'm going to tee it up where, we'll let Alex briefly give his background. Brad could share a couple of seconds of his background. We're going to basically start top down, work our way through, go macro to micro.

So, Alex, with that, why don't you give the folks here a quick 30-second background? Brad, give them a 30-second background, and then, we'll start jumping right into it.

Alex Pettee: I've been writing on Seeking Alpha since 2015 under Hoya Capital. We've been focused on REITs. So, we cover every U.S.-listed REIT. There's about 160 equity REITs, 40 mortgage REITs.

And I think it's always kind of helpful just to kind of start off with a kind of REIT universe, but we'll first start with Brad.

Brad Thomas: I've been on Seeking Alpha since 2010. Most of you probably know my story, but I've been here almost 15 years. I'm at 14 going on 15 years. During that time, I've been fortunate to start coverage on many different REITs, many of which have become S&P 500 companies today. We've seen a number of M&A transactions. We've seen a lot of these small-caps turn into some mid-caps and some big-caps.

So, it's been great to be on Seeking Alpha and cover the REIT sector. And, again, I'm honored to have Alex and Dave really combine with iREIT. And we're excited about the opportunities and giving investors the best REIT research really on the planet.

AP: Most investors look to REITs for yield. I think that that's something we've kind of learned over time is that most investors aren't as interested as us in the on the ground property level trends. It's an income vehicle and so, REITs have kind of assumed that role as a bond-like vehicle. That comes with pros and cons.

The one negative side of it is the theme of the REIT rate correlation that REITs are very bond-like. REITs are "rates up, REITs down" trend.

And so, we'll hit on what individual REITs are more rate sensitive, more economically sensitive. We'll look at sorting REITs by market cap tiers. There's very wide differences in dividend yield and in balance sheets, in large-cap, mid-cap, small-cap REITs. And then, we'll hit on some of the trends, like on the private side, how that relates to REITs on the public side.

REITs are really very interesting because it's the only kind of dual market where there's a public side and there's a private side. There's two different valuations of these assets of the REITs, of the properties held in a REIT structure and on the private side.

And so, that also creates a lot of interesting trends that drive the creation of new REITs through IPOs, through spin-offs, et cetera, or the acquisition of existing REITs.

As of month end, the average equity REIT pays a dividend yield of 3.71%. Average mortgage REIT is north of 10%. One interesting thing here is that this is, of course, the cap-weighted yield, and we'll go through this in a little bit, but this chart here is the breakdown of yield by cap size.

And you'll see that large-cap REITs pay that sub-4% yield, but as you go down that cap tier, mid-cap, small-cap, that you do start to see 5%, 6% yields plus.

Dave, how about, like, we'll kind of kick it off with kind of a "rates up, REITs down" discussion.

DA: Because of that strong employment report that we had last Friday, the acceleration of more Fed rate cuts going to the back half of the year has softened somewhat, maybe 25 basis points going in the rest of the year.

I think another problem that we're seeing here is that as of this moment right now, the 10-year treasury is at 4.065%. Rates are above 4% for the first time in August. And so, again, with people so interest rate sensitive, yield sensitive, REITs have underperformed this week since the Fed rate cut came out.

And this is my opinion. This does not represent the views of Alex or Brad. When you buy REITs, you got to use 25-, 50-year type of lenses. Ignore the day-to-day movement of what's going on in the sector. Focus on the year-to-year big picture. And the reason being, REITs are not day-traded vehicles.

If you want to buy a day-traded vehicle, go out and buy one of the hotel REITs because it's a one-night contract. Then if you want to step it up, then you go into apartments, which are basically six months to one year contracts.

But if you look at the relationship of typical landlord to tenant, it's five-plus year type of relationships that are in place. So, you can't focus on the day-to-day headlines when you've got to look at things in a very big picture. That's just my take, Alex.

DS: Can I jump in real quick? Everybody is, of course, reading about the interest rate cutting cycle that we're in now.

Do you each possibly have an idea of where we're going? Like, how far down do they continue to cut within the end of next year? Do you follow the dot plot maybe, or how do you calculate when you're looking forward?

AP: The rate cut expectations, of course, peaked, the Friday after the September rate cut. At that time, the market was pricing in, I believe, about over five cuts for the year. So that would imply the jumbo in September, it was implying a jumbo in November, and then one in December. That's come back significantly.

We've removed, basically, a full jumbo cut from the rate cut expectations. From about 4.85, which was last week, to about four cuts implied by year-end now.

Really since the end of the great financial crisis, REITs have been extremely interest rate sensitive. So from about 2010 or 2011 through COVID, that REITs were essentially trading as bond vehicles. The closest comparison I'd say to REITs is a corporate bond, a kind of a mid-tier corporate bond.

And so that correlation broke down early pandemic, but then really since the start of the Fed's rate hiking cycle that "rates down, REITs up" or inverse has been a very strong correlation.

A very interesting trend here is that, so REITs were the single worst-performing equity sector from the start of the Fed's rate hiking cycle, that was March 16, 2022. We're the worst through this July.

As soon as we got that early pivot, the REIT sector was the best-performing equity sector from July through about last week, and then REITs have since underperformed. The accumulated underperformance peaked in June of this year at 45 percentage points.

Now that's hugely historic. REITs have historically, on a 10, 15, 20, 25-year basis, basically matched S&P on an annual basis. So to get 45 percentage points of underperformance, that's very significant.

REITs have outperformed by about 10% to 12%, from July until late September, but now, it's back to about a 35%, 36% cumulative underperformance gap that REITs have versus S&P since the start of the Fed rate hiking cycle.

So, I think that it's been a very, very tough couple years for REITs, the rate headwinds have been very strong. And I think the expectation is that same negative force is now positive.

And so it's still very much at the mercy of the Fed, of market interest rates. I think that the easier path now is lower interest rates, and so that's huge for the REIT sector.

DS: Thank you for that, Alex. And, Brad, I would love to hear from you because, I mean, you're the legend, you've graciously given us so much time over the years doing all these webinars here on Seeking Alpha.

You had your start in the commercial space, right? You know the commercial side of it. We can go into all the different industries within the sector, but when I hear about interest rates being cut, and you think about, okay, well, they can refinance, and there's been issues in the commercial space, is there any light at the end of the tunnel for them, if unemployment reports come in okay, job numbers are okay, people continue to spend, GDP continues to grow?

What if we don't get all these cuts that the market is anticipating?

BT: Sure. Well, again, I think it goes back to not only just REITs, but any stock that would cover a publicly-traded company is we look at the cost of capital as a really big important metric to look for.

And within the REIT sector -- again, I was a private developer for 25 years. I'm actually getting back into that business now as well on the private side. And the most of the private developers can't compete with especially a lot of these big names.

We always talk about Realty Income, which is big popular name on Seeking Alpha, ticker symbol (O), or some of these bigger companies, Mid-America Apartments, ticker (MAA), both A-rated balance sheets.

So, we look at the cost of capital and how these publicly-traded companies have much better access to debt. And I think that's what we're really continuing to see.

Again, I'm seeing it now 14, 15 years here on Seeking Alpha, and I think we'll continue to see a lot of these stronger companies, these blue-chip names, continue to grow their earnings and grow their dividends.

Through all of this, through COVID, even through great recession, we saw a number of these companies that were able to continue increasing dividend. So, I think now with this current setup that I think Alex just outlined, and great job, Alex, with that, I think we'll continue to see maybe the rate cuts have softened a bit.

But even if they continue to soften, I mean, we're still seeing those investment spreads. Again, those are the cap rates, minus the cost of capital, those margins continuing to sustain and grow. And so, I think that's really important.

And the great thing about REITs, and I know we're going to get into it here in this webinar talking about some sectors that we like, some sectors we don't like, but the great thing about REITs today, again, as I've been on Seeking Alpha now 15 years, is now you have a much broader way to build portfolios and manage your risk. Certain sectors are going to grow quicker than other sectors.

Cell towers, data centers, none of that existed when I was a developer. There's no way for the individual investor to have access to a data center or cell tower or cannabis REIT or a casino. So, now, there's so many other opportunities to diversify and really define your risk tolerance levels and build these portfolios.

And again, the game is all about cost to capital and scale. I've written about this time and time on Seeking Alpha, but we really like those companies that are able to continue to grow their businesses, see those small-cap companies, we're going to touch on some of those in a minute, turn to large-cap companies.

I think the setup is really attractive right now. Obviously, sentiment, as Alex just said, the last few years have really been brutal, but, again, we're starting to see that rally.

And I think, we'll still continue to have REITs rally, especially, again, those companies that have those wide moat advantages.

DS: David, Alex, maybe you want to start talking us through some of these sectors that we were planning to talk about today?

AP: Some REIT property sectors and even in those property sectors, the individual REITs, there's a very wide range of rate sensitivity, of economic sensitivity.

What the investor should expect at the property sector level, of what they're investing in. Is this REIT extremely rate sensitive? Is this a bond-like vehicle, or is this more of an equity stock-like vehicle?

And then also to what degree that these REITs are good inflation hedges. That typically corresponds with rates, but there are some REITs that are not particularly rate sensitive, but are good hedge vehicles.

So, healthcare REITs, very rate sensitive. Storage REITs, very rate sensitive, very bond-like. Where on the other side of it, you have mall REITs, billboards, these are not rate sensitive. And so, I think that from a portfolio management standpoint, what we basically try to do is try to balance these trends, so we're not making a direct call on interest rates that are not entirely bond-like.

That leads to an interesting discussion on REIT valuations. And so, obviously, what we've seen at this REIT rebound since early July, for the first time since about mid-2021, that REITs are now trading at NAV premiums. And this is important from that kind of first point that I talked about in this dual market structure, right?

There's a private real estate industry, and there's a public real estate industry. And a lot of the flow between new REIT creations and REITs being acquired is driven by this NAV premium. So, what NAV basically is, it's: are the real estate assets held by these REITs more valuable in the REIT structure or less valuable in the REIT structure?

When they're more valuable at the REIT sector level that the flow is into public REITs, that there's new REITs being created, there's private entities that are launching REITs. And when this is a NAV discount that the flow is out, that there's more acquisitions, because you can basically buy the same private property in a REIT structure for cheaper than going out.

And so, yes, what we've seen the last couple of years is this net outflow of REITs being taken private. In 2021, there was about 215 REITs. There's now about 195.

So one interesting factor with REITs is that the valuations play a meaningful role in how REITs actually operate, that the stock price actually matters from an operational standpoint.

And it all ties back to the cost of capital issue, and that REITs actually perform their best when capital is relatively cheaper. So when REITs are trading at discounts, you really don't get much activity. It's hard to grow externally.

DS: Some REITs have common versus preferred shares. So the question is, how do you approach which asset class you would kind of choose for any individual REIT?

AP: So we also track all of the REIT preferreds, 174 individual REIT preferred stocks. The average yield on these is about 7.9%, right? So there is a significant yield premium on the REIT preferreds.

BT: Not every REIT, of course, has preferreds. I'll recommend, and I don't know about some of those on the team, but I like to have a small percentage of those, kind of as Alex pointed out, as yield enhancers. I like around 10%, 20%, but, again, everybody has got their own risk tolerance level.

And a lot of these companies do have issued preferred, but not all, and there are some really attractive preferreds as well. So, anyway, hopefully, that answers that question.

And there's actually a couple preferred REIT ETFs that all they do is just invest in REIT preferreds, and that may be another angle as well.

AP: Since 2019, the REIT common has outperformed the REIT preferreds. It definitely is kind of a different type of vehicle. It's certainly much more yield focused.

Essentially, all of the REIT preferreds are cumulative, and so it's very rare to kind of have major distribution issues. There have been a handful of cases where the preferred dividend cut as part of a acquisition, right? So, that'd be Cedar (CDR.PR.B) and the Wheeler (WHLR) situation.

But the vast majority of REIT preferreds stayed current on their distribution throughout COVID, and all except, I believe, the Wheeler and the Cedar are now current on their preferreds.

DS: So, I want to go ahead and steer the ship a little to a different industry. Brad, you mentioned cell towers and there's industrials and data centers and all these other ones, but I think the one that always comes to the top of mind when people talk about real estate is they think about their home.

They think about the consumer. They think about how interest rates are affecting the consumer. So, in the mortgage industry, could you give us a lay of the land and maybe what you're looking at housing when it comes to maybe even private equity companies are going in there and kind of adjusting some values and things like that, what are we seeing within mortgage, multifamily, and that sort of area?

AP: Yeah, I think that housing theme has always been kind of our major focus. I think that was kind of, like, when we started publishing here in late 2010s, housing was what we saw as the most compelling kind of macro real estate theme.

What was driving this was post great financial crisis, this essentially near shutdown of new construction. So, you saw historically low levels of new construction starting in about 2010, and that kind of built up this significant housing shortage. And I think, in 2018, 2019, that term "housing shortage" was not used often, right? It wasn't in the daily conversation as it is now.

What we are basically forecasting is that this underbuilding was basically coming in front of this big wave, right? So, I guess, it's Gen Z or the largest age cohort today is that 30- to 36-year-old age cohort. And so, back in 2018, 2019, we kind of saw this as being the significant catalyst, right? There were this clash of high demand and historically low supply.

And, of course, what we didn't forecast when we're making these calls in 2018, 2019 was how COVID would kind of accelerate these trends. And instead of playing out over 10, 15 years, it basically played out in a six month to 18 month time period where you had all this housing demand clashing with this historically low housing supply.

I think that bullish housing case is still intact. And I think that what goes on the housing side, also on the commercial side, it's all about the supply side is really, I think, where really the area to kind of focus on, right?

The supply takes years and years to kind of build, and in many cases in some property sectors that there's structural constraints to new supply. On the single family side, of course, there's zoning, et cetera, that creates these barriers to new supply growth.

And so, those are, I think, if you look at the outperforming commercial sectors you'll find that the supply theme is really the overriding trend and that the serial outperformers tend to be those not with necessarily high demand, but it's that supply constraints.

And it's kind of functioning in that Goldilocks zone where it's not robust enough that the prices get bid up, that there's a lot of new construction. But it's the kind of sectors that are under the radar, so aren't getting huge supply growth, but can outperform just kind of based on that -- those structural trends.

DS: Brad, I wanted to see, do you have a thought about this whole mortgage and, specifically, I mean, the people out there obviously trying to still buy homes.
